title: Appeals 101
desc: HLR, Supplemental, or Board -- which lane to pick, with form numbers, timelines, and strategy

sections.heading: :flag: The Three Appeal Lanes Under AMA
sections.body:
Got a denial? You have **three shots** under the Appeals Modernization Act (AMA). Each one is a different tool for a different situation -- pick wrong and you waste months.

Under `38 CFR 3.2500`, here are your options:

| Lane | Form | What It Does |
|------|------|--------------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | `VA Form 20-0995` | Submit **new and relevant evidence** for a fresh review |
| **Higher-Level Review** | `VA Form 20-0996` | A **senior reviewer** re-examines the same evidence for errors |
| **Board of Veterans' Appeals** | `VA Form 10182` | A **Veterans Law Judge** reviews your case |

> [!important] Each lane has different rules, different timelines, and different strategic advantages. Choosing the right one depends on **why you were denied** and **what evidence you have**.

> [!check] Before picking a lane, read your denial letter carefully. The **stated reasons for denial** tell you exactly which lane gives you the best shot.

sections.heading: :doc: Supplemental Claims: When You Have New Evidence
sections.body:
This is your go-to when you have **new evidence that wasn't previously considered**. File `VA Form 20-0995` and bring the goods.

**What counts as new and relevant evidence:**
- A new **medical opinion** or **nexus letter**
- Updated **treatment records** showing worsening
- A **buddy statement** corroborating your claim
- Newly obtained **service records**
- A new **diagnosis** addressing the denial reason

> [!important] The evidence must be "new and relevant" under `38 CFR 3.2501` -- it has to **directly address the reason your claim was denied**. If you were denied for no nexus, a new nexus letter is relevant. If denied for no current diagnosis, a new diagnosis is relevant.

**Key stats:**
- **No limit** on how many you can file
- **Average processing time:** 4-6 months
- Each filing just needs to identify or submit new and relevant evidence

> [!tip] VA's **full duty to assist** under `38 CFR 3.159` applies to Supplemental Claims. That means VA will help obtain records and may order new C&P exams. This is a major advantage over Higher-Level Review.

> [!check] If you have ANY new evidence that addresses the denial reason, this is almost always your best first move.

sections.heading: :search: Higher-Level Review: When VA Made an Error
sections.body:
VA messed up? A **senior reviewer examines the same record** with fresh eyes. No new evidence allowed -- file `VA Form 20-0996`.

**HLR is your move when:**
- VA **misapplied a regulation** or diagnostic code
- The rater **ignored favorable evidence** already in your file
- There was a **clear and unmistakable error** in the decision
- VA **failed its duty to assist** (didn't obtain records, didn't provide an adequate exam)
- The rating **doesn't match the evidence** already on record

> [!check] **Request the informal conference call** -- always take this option. It lets you speak directly with the senior reviewer and highlight the specific errors you've identified. This is your chance to say "look at page 47 of my C-file."

> [!warning] No new evidence means NO new evidence. Don't bring a new nexus letter to an HLR -- that gets rejected. If you have new evidence, file a Supplemental Claim instead.

**Processing time:** 2-4 months average

> [!tip] If the HLR reviewer finds a **duty-to-assist error**, the claim gets sent back to the regional office for correction. This is a common win scenario -- VA didn't do its job the first time, and the senior reviewer catches it.

sections.heading: :gavel: Board of Veterans' Appeals: The Big Guns
sections.body:
When the lower levels have failed you, or your case involves **complex legal issues**, bring it to a Veterans Law Judge. File `VA Form 10182`.

**The Board has three dockets:**

| Docket | New Evidence? | Hearing? | Avg. Timeline |
|--------|--------------|----------|----------------|
| **Direct Review** | No | No | 6-12 months |
| **Evidence Submission** | Yes (90 days) | No | 12-18 months |
| **Hearing** | Yes (90 days after) | Yes | 18-24+ months |

> [!tip] The **Hearing docket** has the highest win rate. Yes, the wait is longer, but you get to tell your story directly to the judge. If your case has any complexity or nuance, this is worth the wait.

> [!important] Board decisions carry **more legal weight** and can set precedent. A BVA grant is harder for VA to overturn than a regional office grant.

> [!check] If you've already been denied at HLR or Supplemental, the Board is your next stop. Don't give up -- many cases are won at the Board that were denied below.

sections.heading: :alert: Deadlines That Matter
sections.body:
This is where claims die. Miss a deadline, lose your money. It's that simple.

> [!danger] You have **ONE YEAR** from the date of your VA decision to file in any appeal lane while preserving your effective date. This is non-negotiable.

**The effective date math:**

| When You File | Effective Date | Back Pay? |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Within 1 year** | Original claim date (or ITF date) | **Full back pay** from that date |
| **After 1 year** | New filing date | **No back pay** from original period |

> [!warning] You can still file a Supplemental Claim after one year (there's no time limit on those), but your effective date **resets to the new filing date**. You lose all the back pay from the original period. That could be tens of thousands of dollars.

For Board appeals:
- File `VA Form 10182` **within one year**
- Evidence Submission/Hearing docket: **90 days** after filing to submit new evidence

> [!check] **Never let a deadline pass without action.** If you need more time to gather evidence, file your appeal NOW and submit evidence as it becomes available. An Intent to File or a filed form with evidence to follow beats a missed deadline every time.

sections.heading: :target: Which Lane to Choose: A Decision Framework
sections.body:
Stop guessing. Walk through this decision tree.

**Question 1: Do you have new evidence that addresses the denial reason?**
- **Yes** --> File a **Supplemental Claim** (`20-0995`)
- **No** --> Move to Question 2

**Question 2: Did VA make a clear error with the existing evidence?**
- **Yes** --> File a **Higher-Level Review** (`20-0996`)
- **No** --> Move to Question 3

**Question 3: Is your case legally complex, or denied at lower levels?**
- **Yes** --> File with the **Board** (`10182`) -- consider the Hearing docket

> [!tip] **You can lane-switch.** If your HLR is denied, file a Supplemental Claim with new evidence. If your Supplemental is denied, file with the Board. Each new decision starts a **fresh one-year clock** for the next filing.

> [!quote] Real scenario: Veteran denied for no nexus. Files HLR (wrong move -- no new evidence to fix the nexus gap). HLR denied. Then files Supplemental with a new nexus letter from a private doctor. Granted. The HLR cost 3 months, but the lane-switch saved the claim.

> [!check] When in doubt, ask yourself: "What was the specific reason for denial, and do I have something new to address it?" That answer tells you the lane.

sections.heading: :lock: Clear and Unmistakable Error (CUE)
sections.body:
CUE is the **nuclear option** -- it attacks a **final VA decision** and can unlock **years or decades of back pay**.

Under `38 CFR 3.105(a)`, if you prove a prior final decision contained clear and unmistakable error, VA must revise the decision and pay you back to the original effective date.

**CUE requires proving ALL THREE elements:**

1. The **correct facts** as known at the time were not before the adjudicator, OR the **law was incorrectly applied**
2. The error was **undebatable** -- reasonable minds could not differ
3. The error was **outcome-determinative** -- but for the error, the result would have been different

> [!danger] CUE is an extremely high bar. It is NOT a disagreement with how VA weighed the evidence. It is NOT "they should have rated me higher." It's a **provable, undebatable mistake** in fact or law.

> [!warning] If you file a CUE motion and lose, it can actually hurt future arguments. Don't file CUE on a hunch or frustration.

> [!check] If you believe you have a CUE claim, **consult an experienced VA attorney** before filing. The stakes are enormous (potentially decades of back pay) and the standard is demanding. This is not a DIY claim.

sections.heading: :shield: Beyond BVA: The Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims
sections.body:
If the Board denies you, there's one more level: the **U.S. Court of Appeals for Veterans Claims (CAVC)**. This is a federal court that reviews BVA decisions for **legal errors**.

> [!danger] You have **120 days** from the date of the BVA decision to file a Notice of Appeal with the CAVC. This deadline is **jurisdictional** -- miss it and the court literally cannot hear your case. No exceptions.

**What to know about CAVC:**
- Typically **requires an attorney** (many work on contingency)
- The court can **affirm, reverse, or remand** the BVA decision
- Most successful appeals result in a **remand** -- sending the case back to BVA with instructions to fix the legal error

> [!important] Landmark CAVC cases that shaped veterans' rights:
- **Nieves-Rodriguez v. Peake** (No. 06-312) -- medical opinion adequacy standards
- **Jandreau v. Nicholson** (No. 04-1254) -- lay evidence competency
- **Euzebio v. McDonough** (No. 17-2879) -- TDIU evidence requirements

> [!tip] If you're considering a CAVC appeal, contact a veterans' law attorney immediately after the BVA denial. The 120-day clock starts ticking the day the decision is mailed, and you need time to find and retain counsel.

sections.heading: :chart: Timeline Expectations
sections.body:
Set realistic expectations so you're not calling the VA hotline every week.

| Appeal Lane | Average Processing Time | Notes |
|-------------|------------------------|-------|
| **Supplemental Claim** | 4-6 months | Faster with strong, straightforward evidence |
| **Higher-Level Review** | 2-4 months | Fastest -- no new evidence development needed |
| **Board (Direct Review)** | 6-12 months | Fastest Board docket |
| **Board (Evidence Submission)** | 12-18 months | Mid-range |
| **Board (Hearing)** | 18-24+ months | Longest wait, highest win rate |
| **CAVC Appeal** | 12-24 months | After filing Notice of Appeal |

> [!warning] These are **averages** -- individual cases vary significantly. Complex cases with multiple issues take longer. Simple single-issue claims can resolve faster.

> [!check] **Track your claim** on VA.gov and contact your representative if it stalls beyond the expected timeframe. Squeaky wheel gets the grease -- but give it reasonable time first.

> [!tip] While waiting, keep building your evidence. Get current treatment, document symptoms, and collect buddy statements. If you win a remand, you'll be ready to hit the ground running.
